blurb: A 'what's this' explainer page for an online project called Option Repro, filed here under reproductive justice — the page's actual content and purpose aren't recoverable from the title or bare URL alone.
ambiguous_note: Title is just the site's own URL; without visiting the live page there's no further clue to what the project actually is.

blurb: USAID's introductory e-learning course on how international development programs and institutions can meaningfully include LGBTQI+ people and perspectives in their design and delivery.
A podcast episode built around Eve Tuck and K. Wayne Yang's landmark argument that decolonization means the literal repatriation of land and life, not a metaphor for other social justice work.
ambiguous_note: The episode title clearly nods to Tuck & Yang's well-known essay of the same name, but this podcast's own framing and guests aren't confirmable from the title alone.

blurb: A New York Times report on the emotional toll that repeated exposure to news, images, and conversation about racialized policing and violence takes on Black children.
A Center for American Progress report linking the chronic stress mass incarceration inflicts on Black families and communities to elevated rates of Black infant mortality.
An essay arguing that racial equity work is unsustainable without attending to practitioners' own well-being — that personal healing is infrastructure for the work, not a distraction from it.

title_clean: Jonathan Holloway: Jim Crow Wisdom — Memory and Identity in Black America since 1940
blurb: A recorded lecture by historian Jonathan Holloway on how Black Americans have remembered, mythologized, and made meaning from the Jim Crow era across generations.
A curated roundup of reports, toolkits, and funds for businesses looking to take concrete action on closing the racial wealth gap.
ambiguous_note: Framed as a roundup/resource list rather than a single document; its exact contents aren't specified in the title.

blurb: A collection of dialogues in which Angela Davis discusses the meaning of freedom across abolition, feminism, and prison reform, extending arguments from her broader body of work.

title_clean: Korintha Mitchell, "Identifying White Mediocrity and Know-Your-Place Aggression: A Form of Self-Care," African American Review
blurb: An academic essay naming 'know-your-place aggression' — the everyday ways white mediocrity is protected and enforced in institutions — and framing the act of identifying it as a form of self-care.

title_clean: Robin Bernstein, Racial Innocence: Performing American Childhood and Race from Slavery to Civil Rights
blurb: A scholarly history of how the idea of childhood innocence was racially constructed in America — reserved for white children while Black children were routinely denied it — from slavery through the civil rights era.

title_clean: Repairing the Whole: How Reparations Can Address Physical and Mental Health
blurb: A Nonprofit Quarterly piece arguing that reparations should be understood not only as economic repair but as a public health intervention, given the documented physical and mental health toll of racial harm.
